speaker: interviewer
text: 15Th of May, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. It's rolling, and camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started telling me about the punishments that you received from the kapos. Could you continue with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One day, we were-- I was so cold that I just couldn't stand it out in the quarry. So I pinched two bags of cement-- bags, the papers only. And I put it underneath to keep me warm. And I forgot to take it off when I left work. And they, they found it on the way back because I was a little bit chestier than when I left. And I got a punishment, 25 lashes, front of the-- front of the Fluechtling. But I can't remember more than five or six because I, I passed out. And the lashes was because if we put bags on us, the lices will get us. But the paper get lice from us, not we get lice from the paper. And that was that. So for about two or three days, I couldn't go to work. And this little Wehrmacht sergeant or whatever was his-- he came into the lager looking for me why I wasn't working for two, three days. And he found me in my block with bruises and whatever you get from six lashes. The guys told me I got only six, because I can't remember too much. And they wanted to show to the rest of them that you don't put-- it's like a punishment front of everybody. And he came in, and he brought a piece of bread, and told that German guy who was the blockalteste or a kapo who was the meanest things, a sadist-- a definite sadist because he did things to us which I don't even think the Germans would ask him to do. He was a German prisoner, of criminal or what. He made us run barefoot at 2 o'clock the night, naked in the block, all of us. Then we ran back. Then he came 4 o'clock up, whatever we were covered with, not much. I don't know what the cover was-- a blanket, or just the remnants of a blanket. And then he said, you have to go to the toilet. And I said, we don't have to go. Yes, you have to. And then you had to go out, and you have to put on your things, the stripe. Mostly we slept in the stripe. But sometimes it wasn't allowed. When he was in a bad mood, it was not allowed to sleep in the stripe. Then he sent us out to the toilet in the night, 4 o'clock the morning in the-- in the winter because that was already winter up there. That was October, November. Anyway, the culmination of the whole lager is that so few people left after, after September, October, November. Beginning of December, they have to evacuate the lager because there wasn't enough people to work. They then brought new ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were left at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 200 of us. I didn't count one by one, but approximately 200 because I don't think so we needed more than two wagon to take us wherever they evacuated us. Later on, I knew we were taken to Dachau. So from here, they shipped us to Dachau. And we arrived in Dachau at night time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By freight train. Again, filled up the train. I was already a little bit-- [LAUGHS] a little bit down on my physique-- the, the well-built guy become a, a walking skeleton, but still had muscles, and I still had energy. But I was approximately 35 kilos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the kapos travel with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't know because they didn't ship them with us. They didn't come with us in the-- in the wagon. And when we got to Dachau, I didn't see them. I only saw the same Fluechtling with us who I was with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain Fluechtling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fluechtling, that's a German version of prisoner. And my German developed in there. I learned a lot. I learned Yiddish there, or understand Yiddish. If I answered, I answered German. But from the Polish prisoner, but we didn't call ourselves prisoners. We were Fluechtlings. And we talked only German because if we had to go every morning at, I don't know, five or six o'clock to the platz, to counted, the appellplatz, appellplatz. Appellplatz we called them where came the German boss, and we had to stand in-- I don't know, tens or fives in line. And they went through and counted. And we stand on the appellplatz sometimes for two hours, three hours, and freezing because purposely, he didn't came. He wanted more and more, and people collapsed there and died there. And then we got there and started burying them. And all I know is when they ask you a question, all you said is, Herr Sturmfuhrer or Herr Obersturmfuhrer, mein name ist Julius Fischer. Mein nummer ist [GERMAN]. That was the tape which we said to every German whoever we met. That was the only things I have ever spoken to a German. Mein name ist Julius Fischer. Mein nummer ist [GERMAN]. That's how I know my number, which I got in Auschwitz, is not in my arm, because I was very young. And I came in the very end part of the Auschwitz time, so they didn't put the number in. But they gave us the number, and you had to know it. And if you wake me up 4 o'clock in the morning, I can't tell you in any other language but in German. If you asked me in Hungarian, it takes me half an hour for my mind to translate that all to English. And we were Fluechtlings. That's all I know. That's a prisoner, or whatever it is. It's, it's a German word which describes a bloody Jew in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived to Dachau. And in Dachau, whatever little hair had grown out because from the lack of food, the hair had grown very, very slowly, and also in winter. We got again shaved everywhere. And then again, we had a shower, and they came this disinfectant stuff. We got, again, a new stripe prison clothe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put us into barracks, but the barracks were civilized. Two of us were sleeping one underneath, one over. The rooms were clean. They were room, not barracks, stone walls, rooms. It had a special shower section, decent toilets. And all we did there was lying there, doing nothing. And we even got some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bread, the usual food, but some, something more. I think so we got sixth of bread, a bit more butter, a bit more marmalade, and the same soup what we always get. And that was the food in my whole nearly year of, of imprisonment. That was the general menu. There was no, no other stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the children from the children's barracks in Auschwitz, were any of them still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I became an adult the minute I left Auschwitz and became a worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever asked your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But it showed, that's why I think so the German, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this, this Wehrmacht officer took a sorry on me because I was young and I was still very, very virile as far as working concerned. And he was-- he must have been a grandfather by that time because he was white-haired, an old Wehrmacht soldier. And he brought me food more than what was the normal thing. And I could eat the remnants which the German soldiers left in the container. I could eat that. And that was bean soup sometimes, sometimes a little meat and so forth. And that kept me a little bit better condition than the rest of them. But as far as the youngsters, I met new people, new-- I was huddling with new people together-- you-- to huddle and you know, twice you come together. Some were in the work or some were in, you know, [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH] or whatever it is because it was no surname. It's only Christian name we knew each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about amongst yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. Food and when we get out, what will be the first food. And I can tell you my menu. My menu was a Berliner with an egg on it baked underneath so the Berliner curls up, and then you put an egg in it, and make a scrambled eggs, and poppy seed noodle. The egg was the main course, and the poppy seed noodle was the, the dessert. And that's what we talked about for nearly three quarter of a year. Somebody had different ideas. The saliva was coming exactly the same way because you felt you eat what he said, and he felt he eat what you said. And I'm not sentimental about it. It is a fact. And this is-- world happenings, I only remember one. In Auschwitz, we heard the news that they committed a plot against Hitler. And we thought the war is over, because when they said there's a plot against Hitler happened, OK. We thought it's over. And then they said the plot wasn't successful. And later on I heard that was the plot. But when Rommel and a few generals wanted to get rid of Hitler, that's the only news you heard. In the work, you didn't hear anything. I worked in this-- in this [NON-ENGLISH] section of the German expedition of mine where I only saw breathing holes coming up from the ground. And then we knew-- and, and trucks-- motorized-- electrically motorized trucks so you couldn't even hear were going down and up in paved roads. And then they disappeared. And that's where we knew that something is underground, because we all see workers, German workers, in this electricalized buggies carrying certain stuff somewhere. We didn't see the entrance. We didn't see how they come out. All we saw, up from the earth, breathing holes like you have now here, the plumbers give you breathing holes when they build a building for the smell or whatever to go out. All the other things was outside. The railroad we built was we had to-- after we worked in the stone quarry, they break the stone in certain size. And then when, when we didn't have-- day was bad, we couldn't work on the stone mine, they took us out to the railway line. And they had a hammer, which looked like the hammershark's head. And with that, we had to hit the stone underneath the, the logs which carries the railway line. I don't know how they call this little piece of very heavy thing, but it's-- and to make it level, you have to put hammer stones underneath. So when the train comes, it's on a solid ground. It doesn't sink, and it is level. And that's all I knew about it. The guys we worked with, we had only energy to look after yourself that you arrived to the place alive, and you go back to the barracks alive. It was no talking. It was no-- it was no conversation, no topics. The only thing when we talked was Sunday when we ate. Everybody thought his menu, and that was our food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me how you heard about the news of the plot [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard in, in Birkenau at Auschwitz. Whether they got it from outside-- because in Auschwitz, the blockalteste, certain kapos, they had connection with the German guards. And they were in a privileged position. So certain news came in, and no news went out because the only news was inside is when you die or when you don't. It was no, no topics or was nothing. But from outside, certain news came. I know people find their families in different lager because those days we call it organizing. It sound in English strange, but in German, everything was organization, Organisation. The food, if you get extra food, you was organizing something. If you get extra clothes, you could buy from somebody for a cigarette or for, for rolls-- tobacco or something-- you were organizing. Everything was organizing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people organized to send message out that so and so is here. Ask the ladies, the woman barracks, if anybody know by that name. And that's how families sometimes find each other. The man was in the man's, the woman was in there, and the child was maybe somewhere else. In Auschwitz, you had about six, seven, or maybe ten different lagers, and, and 25 barracks on every lager. It was a huge concentration camp. So once people were able to contact to each other, were they able to keep up that contact in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they had some source of Organisation going, which means they had something to bribe the guards or the kapos, they could. If you didn't have anything, you couldn't do anything. And, and that's it. So, so I'm going back to Dachau now. [NON-ENGLISH], the wife, is here. So in evening, we are-- got naked, got showered. We got into the blocks, and we got food. Later on, we learned the reason they put us into this section because that was the section for the Red Cross to visit. And we were like showpieces. Show that it doesn't matter how bad conditions the Fluechtling are, the German regime looks after them. Have a look how they live, their conditions, so and so and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: But God gave me again the signal that I said, that can't go on too long. We are eating. We are sleeping here nicely. What for? We don't do anything. I want to do work because I had a basic instinct that as long as I work, I am alive. They won't put me in to go into the ovens. How often--

speaker: interviewee
text: And by that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. How often did the Red Cross visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came I think so twice. I wasn't too long in there. I was there about two weeks, three weeks. And I regained a little bit of condition. So when the next organization came to organize volunteers, I volunteered again. And this time they put us onto a truck-- about 100, 120 of us, or I don't know how many, a truck full of Fluechtlings, open truck. And I know we already-- we were in Dachau because we knew the Dachau. But from there, they took us to Ausberg, which is a large city in Germany. And that was the first place where I was a human being again for a couple of weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By, by truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In months you mean? That was still in December because I arrived in Dachau in the beginning of December, and I left Ausberg at the end of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944. And they took us to the Messerschmitt airplane factory in Ausberg in the city. We lived in the city in a hangar. A big airplane hangar was transformed into a living where about in my estimation, about two, two and half thousand people lived in the hangar with bunkers. We got in the job little vouchers for which vouchers we could buy-- we had a sort of a canteen in the hangar. We didn't have stripes. We had civil clothes. But from the civil clothes, the back of the clothes, a big square was cut out, and the foreigner material was put in. That's how the German workers with whom we were knew that were Fluechtlings and not Germans. Otherwise, we were taken to the factory, which was very close to this hangar. And I was working. I had a hammer, and I was flattening, or evening, the wings of the airplane.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other prisoners were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot. A lot. The factory was huge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't have the faintest. I tell you, I wouldn't be able to even guess because it was so many sections. It was an ordinary factory where they manufactured airplanes. It was not for Fluechtlings. It was for the German workers working there, tradespeople. We were the laborers. And we did manual job. They did the skilled job. And they didn't talk to us, but we were working practically next to each other. And God wasn't with me that time because I developed a boil on my side, a huge boil, which had to be cut. And they took me into the emergency room of the factory, and they cut the boil, and I couldn't work. So I, I went back to the hangar, and I just hanged around. And because I didn't do anything, there was another, what we call, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] is a sicky, like a sicky, in German called [GERMAN]. And the reason I know the name because they took us from there to a [GERMAN] lager. The [GERMAN] lager is a sicky lager where all you had to do was dying. You hardly had anything to eat, but you didn't do anything. And that's what I was scared of. And because of my boil, they put us on a truck about-- again, 40 or 50 of us. And not far away from Ausberg, it took about half a day by car, or maybe a day. And we arrived to a lager, a set of lagers, which called Kaufering. And in my days in Kaufbeuren, I heard about Kaufering because people who worked there with me-- who were Fluechtling with me-- they were from a lot of other lagers, not just from Auschwitz. And they were in Kaufering. And they said it was a fantastic lager. You could get potato there. You could go out to the field to get potatoes. You had warmers on which you can bake the potato because that was surviving time. I mean, you didn't talk about clothes. You talked about food. Food was surviving or not. Even work didn't matter. It was no matter what you do as long as you get something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Kaufering. And I said, my God, I heard this Kaufering. That's a good lager. But Kaufering become a [GERMAN] lager, which meant that everybody from around this area, [NON-ENGLISH], in Germany got concentrated at Kaufering to die there, which meant that you stayed in the barracks. You wake up whenever you wanted. You went, go outside when you wanted. You got about 24 of us got a kilo of bread-- if it was a kilo, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the marmalade which was, let's say, ten for one-- sorry, one for ten. It became twenty for-- one for twenty, marmalade and margarine. And the soup become water-ish. No more pumpkin in it, just the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in about-- I got there in December, January, February. By March, I was a walking corpse. I was become a muselmann, which meant that your energy only allowed you to drag your foot. You couldn't lift your legs up. You only could drag. And the bunkers were half underground, so we ate the grass which grown on the top of the bunker and waited for when the extinction comes. Who was in charge of you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. The guards, we don't know because I had no energy to know anything. We had no energy to walk from one barrack to another. All we did, just hang around. And I became, I think so, 20 to 22 kilos. And what you see on the films since then, I was one of them. The only thing is I was still alive. And, and I walked like this until around March, end of March, beginning of April, we started hearing bombardments, guns, heavy guns. And when we walked out of the barracks, we could see smokes in the horizon. And we were still mentally a bit alert, and we knew that the front is coming closer and closer. And about 20-- I got free 29, 28, 27-- around 26 or 27 of April, they evacuated the lagers because Kaufering had no ovens, no crematorium. They were what we called dying lagers. You died by natural death. You didn't have to-- to be put to gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How big?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in lager five, and I heard of seven. Could be from one to nine. And this was a full of people. So they took us into-- by this time we carried each other. We just-- and they put us into freight wagons again. And the wagon left in the evening, I don't know the day and the date. And next morning, a tremendous Fliegalarm, which means air raid, came on the wagons. And we were stopped in the open fields. And as we looked out in the barbed wire through the windows, next to us was a German set of carriages. But they were army transportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the carriages they had big guns. What they called? Anti-aircraft, that's the correct-- anti-aircraft guns. But the size? Enormous, not ordinary guns. They were mounted on the railways, which means when they shot, they made a shot, the whole railway was moving back five or six meters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why we got the, the air raid because most probably English because they very small bombard-- not the-- not the Liberator, the heavy because we knew, and we knew the [? F-2s ?] because in Kaufbeuren and in Dachau we heard, when the F-2s were passing us. We didn't know. We just heard noise which was absolutely out of ordinary.

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't see anything, just vroom-- what we hear now when a jet passing. So and they saw the tremendously big anti-gun columns. An air raid game. And they bombard it to pieces. And with them, they bombarded our section too. So out of, let's say, we had 10 rail, maybe two or three survived. The rest of them was-- and how? I do not know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the door opened, and we could come out. And was no Germans. The next, the Germans was hanging out, bodies-- our railway truck was bodies from the bombardment, hundreds of bodies lying all around. And we started putting out-- whatever left, we start putting out to the open field. And suddenly we found nothing-- no guards, no nothing. And we found some sort of food in the German section. Because as they bombarded, they opened up-- pardon me. They had tins, and they had something, biscuits and so forth. So we picked up some sort of a food and start eating. And the instinct for life, we didn't talk. We just looked each other. And we started walking on the open field to watch the smoke. And walking-- I mean, we were dragging, maybe a kilometer a morning and a kilometer in the afternoon. I don't know what distance we made.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the other people with you in the same physical condition as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah, yeah. By that time, my extra, what I had in Dachau and from a little bit extra food left on me was gone, and I became the same muselmann than the rest. And we started walking towards or sliding or however you want to call it towards the smoke and it started getting dark. And as it got dark, we start seeing [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not cars, not even tanks, but vehicles coming towards us with caterpillar back and rubber front, which mean it wasn't heavy, heavy tanks. It was a sort of a transportation vehicles of Germans. We didn't know. We thought maybe it's the freeing forces of-- we didn't know, American or Russian or whoever it is. We just know that the front is there, and, and the heavy shooting. So when they started coming, the felt that that's it. We got freed. And suddenly, they started shooting at us. And the way we know the bullet was intended to us because in the darkness, we could see every fourth or fifth bullet was fluorescent. You could see the bullet coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went on the ground until they surrounded us. It was not more than 150 people or, I don't know, 80 people or 300 people. We were all lied down and waited what's coming. And they made us start walking back to the-- towards the railway line. And we didn't know. By that time, they repaired the railway line, and they brought new carriages. Didn't need much, two or three carriages. And we walked back again. I don't know what time we arrived back. How far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few kilometers. How much could we walk in a day time? Practically zilch-- maybe a kilometer an hour, not even that. Maybe 500 meter an hour. By the time we came back, it was night in earnest. Could have been the mornings.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put us back to the truck again, locked the door. And we go back to a place which already was familiar to me. It was Dachau. And started the same procedure-- shower again, this disinfectant stuff, and into the blocks, exactly the same blocks where we where when the Red Cross came.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape four of the interview with Jules Forgacs. It's the 16th of May 18995, and we are in Sydney, Australia. [INAUDIBLE].
